title: Complex Output Tracing
description: Develop the step-by-step mental execution model needed to predict output from programs involving inheritance chains, constructor sequences, dynamic dispatch, and casting — the hardest question type in CSE110 exams.

sections.content: 🎯 Why This Chapter Is the Hardest in CSE110

Complex output questions combine *every* OOP concept at once: constructor chaining, static vs instance dispatch, method overriding, `super` calls, reference vs object type, and casting. Missing any one rule produces wrong output. This chapter teaches a **systematic trace protocol** that eliminates guesswork and catches the subtle interactions between these rules.
📋 The 5-Step Trace Protocol
Before predicting any output, apply these steps in order for every statement in `main`:

items: **Identify object type** — what class was instantiated with `new`? This is the *actual type* and never changes.
**Identify reference type** — what type is the variable declared as? This controls which methods are *visible*.
**Constructor chain** — when `new` runs, trace upward: every constructor calls `super()` (implicitly or explicitly) before its own body runs.
**Method dispatch** — for overridden instance methods, always use the **actual (runtime) type**. For static methods or fields, use the **reference (compile-time) type**.
**`super` keyword in methods** — `super.method()` bypasses overriding and calls the *parent*'s version regardless of actual type.

sections.label: 🚨 The Rule That Breaks Everything If Forgotten
sections.content: **Dynamic dispatch applies to instance methods only.** Static methods, instance variables, and static variables are resolved by **reference type** (compile-time), not actual type. So `Animal a = new Dog(); a.sound()` calls Dog's `sound()`, but `a.name` accesses Animal's `name` field if both declare one — this is **field hiding**, not overriding.
🔗 Rule 1: Constructor Execution Order

Every constructor's first action is calling `super(...)`. If not written explicitly, the compiler inserts `super()` (no-arg). The parent constructor runs **completely** before the child constructor body continues. This means: in a chain `A → B → C` (A is grandparent), creating a `C` object prints A's body first, then B's, then C's.

sections.title: Constructor Chain — Trace the Order

sections.content: **Rule:** Constructors run **outermost parent first, innermost child last**. Think of it as winding up a spring from the top before the child's body can execute.
⚡ Rule 2: Dynamic Dispatch on Overridden Methods

When a method is **overridden** (same name, same parameter list, `@Override`), calling it on a variable always invokes the version belonging to the **actual runtime type**, regardless of the reference type. This is the core of polymorphism.

sections.title: Dynamic Dispatch — Reference Type vs Actual Type

sections.content: 📞 Rule 3: super() in Constructors vs super. in Methods

`super()` in a constructor calls the parent class constructor — it **must be the first statement**. `super.method()` in an instance method calls the parent's version of that method, bypassing the child's override. These are two completely different uses of the `super` keyword.

sections.title: super() in Constructors and super. in Methods

sections.content: 🎭 Rule 4: Calling Overridden Methods from Constructors

This is the most treacherous exam scenario. If a **parent constructor** calls an overridden method, and a **child object** is being constructed, dynamic dispatch means the **child's version** of the method runs — **even though the child constructor hasn't run yet** (so child fields are still at their default values). This produces deeply unintuitive output.

sections.title: Overridden Method Called from Parent Constructor

sections.label: Uninitialized Fields in Overridden Calls
sections.content: When `Base()` calls `show()`, the JVM dispatches to `Derived.show()` because the actual object is `Derived`. But `Derived`'s instance initializers and constructor body haven't run yet — `y` holds its **default value** (0 for int). This is a runtime trap, not a compile error. In exams, whenever you see a constructor calling a non-final method, check whether it's overridden in a subclass.
🔄 Rule 5: Casting and ClassCastException

**Upcasting** (child → parent reference) is always safe and implicit. **Downcasting** (parent → child reference) requires an explicit cast and throws `ClassCastException` at runtime if the actual object isn't of the target type. Use `instanceof` to check before casting.

sections.title: Casting — Safe and Unsafe

sections.content: 🔤 this() Constructor Chaining

Within the same class, one constructor can call another using `this(...)`. Like `super()`, `this()` must be the **first statement**, which means a constructor can call either `this()` **or** `super()`, never both. The chain always ends with a call to a parent constructor.

sections.title: this() Chaining Within a Class

sections.content: **Trace rule for `this()` chains:** The chain runs *deepest* constructor body first, then unwinds back up — the same bottom-up order as constructor chains in inheritance.
🏋️ Practice Exercises

sections.title: Exercise 1: Three-Level Inheritance Chain with super() Calls
sections.explanation:
Trace `new C()`:
1. `C()` is called. Its first statement is `super(5)` → calls `B(5)`.
2. `B(5)` first statement is `super(5)` → calls `A(5)`.
3. `A(5)` prints: `A(5)`. Returns to B(5).
4. `B(5)` continues: prints `B(5)`. Returns to C().
5. `C()` continues: prints `C`.

Note: `B()` (no-arg) is NEVER called. `C()` explicitly calls `super(5)` which maps to `B(int)`, not `B()`. So `B()`'s body (which calls `this(10)`) never executes.

sections.title: Exercise 2: Dynamic Dispatch with Field Hiding
sections.answer:
Hello from Child
Static Parent
Parent
Hello from Child
Static Child
Child
sections.explanation:
p has reference type `Parent`, actual type `Child`.

1. `p.greet()`: instance method, overridden → dynamic dispatch → `Child.greet()` runs. Inside Child.greet(), `name` refers to `Child.name` = "Child". Prints: `Hello from Child`.

2. `p.staticGreet()`: **static method** → resolved by **reference type** = Parent → `Parent.staticGreet()`. Prints: `Static Parent`.

3. `p.name`: **field** → resolved by **reference type** = Parent → `Parent.name` = "Parent". Prints: `Parent`.

4. `c` is the same object cast to `Child`. `c.greet()`: same dynamic dispatch, still prints `Hello from Child`.

5. `c.staticGreet()`: reference type is now `Child` → `Child.staticGreet()`. Prints: `Static Child`.

6. `c.name`: reference type is `Child` → `Child.name` = "Child". Prints: `Child`.

sections.title: Exercise 3: Invalid super() Placement and Wrong Cast
sections.error: Line 8 (Dog constructor): `super("Woof")` must be the **first statement** in the constructor, but `this.breed = breed` appears before it — this is a **compile-time error**. Line 14 (main): `(Dog) a` — `a` actually holds a `Cat` object. Casting a `Cat` to `Dog` is a **runtime error** (ClassCastException), because Cat and Dog are sibling classes (neither is a subtype of the other).
sections.explanation: Two distinct bugs: (1) **Compile-time error** — `super()` or `this()` must always be the very first statement in any constructor. Placing `this.breed = breed` before `super("Woof")` violates this rule; the compiler rejects it with 'call to super must be first statement in constructor'. (2) **Runtime error** — `ClassCastException`. Although `Cat` and `Dog` both extend `Animal`, they are siblings in the hierarchy. A `Cat` object reference can only be safely cast to `Animal` or `Cat`, never to `Dog`. The `instanceof` operator would return `false` here. The fix assigns a `Dog` object to `a` so the downcast is valid.

sections.title: Exercise 4: Overridden Method Called from Parent Constructor
sections.answer:
Alpha val=100
Beta.display val=0
Beta val=200
Beta.display val=200
200
sections.explanation:
Trace `new Beta()`:
1. `Beta()` implicitly calls `super()` → `Alpha()` runs.
2. In `Alpha()`: prints `Alpha val=100` (Alpha.val=100 is initialized before the constructor body runs via field initializer).
3. `Alpha()` calls `display()`. Dynamic dispatch: actual type is `Beta` → `Beta.display()` runs. But `Beta`'s constructor hasn't executed yet, and `Beta.val`'s field initializer `= 200` hasn't run yet either. `Beta.val` is at its default: **0**. Prints: `Beta.display val=0`.
4. `Alpha()` finishes. Return to `Beta()` constructor body: `Beta.val` is now initialized to 200. Prints: `Beta val=200`.
5. Back in main: `b.display()` → `Beta.display()` with `val=200`. Prints: `Beta.display val=200`.
6. `b.val`: reference type is `Beta` → `Beta.val = 200`. Prints: `200`.

sections.title: Exercise 5: Full Inheritance + Polymorphism Exam Challenge
sections.answer:
X1
Y1
Z1
X2
Y2
show Y1
show Z1
show Y2
2
sections.explanation:
Trace `new Z()` (a = new Z(), actual type Z):
1. Z() → implicit super() → Y() → implicit super() → X().
2. X(): count++ (count=1), id=1, prints `X1`. Return to Y().
3. Y(): prints `Y1` (id=1 from X). Return to Z().
4. Z(): prints `Z1` (id=1). a now references this Z object.

Trace `new Y()` (b = new Y(), actual type Y):
5. Y() → super() → X(): count++ (count=2), id=2, prints `X2`. Return to Y().
6. Y(): prints `Y2`. b references this Y object.

Trace `a.show()` (actual type Z):
7. Z.show() calls `super.show()` first → Y.show(): prints `show Y1` (id=1).
8. Then Z.show() prints `show Z1`.

Trace `b.show()` (actual type Y):
9. Y.show(): prints `show Y2` (id=2).

`X.count` = 2 (one increment per X() constructor call; two objects were created).

sections.content: 📋 Quick Reference Summary

sections.label: Exam Trace Shortcut
sections.content: Draw a two-column table for each object: **Reference Type** | **Actual Type**. For every method call, ask: is it an overridden instance method? If yes → use Actual Type column. Static method or field? → use Reference Type column. This mechanical check eliminates 90% of dynamic dispatch mistakes.

sections.title: Exercise 6: Design a Shape Hierarchy with Polymorphic Area
sections.approach:
1. Declare Shape as abstract with abstract double area().
2. Circle extends Shape: store radius, override area() = Math.PI * r * r.
3. Rectangle extends Shape: store width & height, override area() = w * h.
4. printArea(Shape s) calls s.area() — dynamic dispatch picks the correct subclass.
5. Build Shape[] and iterate.
sections.explanation: Shape is abstract so it cannot be instantiated directly — only concrete subclasses can. Dynamic dispatch: when printArea calls s.area(), the JVM looks at the actual runtime type (Circle or Rectangle) and calls that class's area() implementation. instanceof checks are used here only for labeling; the area() call itself is purely polymorphic. This pattern is the core of the Open/Closed Principle: add new shapes by adding new subclasses, not by modifying printArea. printf format: %.2f rounds to 2 decimal places; Math.PI*25 ≈ 78.5398 → 78.54.

items: **Constructor order**: parent constructor body runs FIRST, child runs AFTER — always outermost parent to innermost child
**Implicit super()**: if no `super()` or `this()` is written, compiler inserts `super()` as first line — parent must have a no-arg constructor or compile error
**super() must be first**: any code before `super()` or `this()` in a constructor is a compile-time error
**this() vs super()**: a constructor can call one or the other, never both — and only as the first statement
**Dynamic dispatch rule**: overridden **instance methods** use **actual (runtime) type** — always
**Static method hiding**: static methods are resolved by **reference (compile-time) type** — no dynamic dispatch
**Field hiding**: instance fields are resolved by **reference type** — fields cannot be polymorphically overridden
**super.method()**: explicitly calls the PARENT class's version of a method, bypasses child's override
**Overridden method in constructor**: if a parent constructor calls an overridden method, the CHILD's version runs — but child fields are uninitialized (default values: 0, null, false)
**Upcasting**: always safe, implicit (`Animal a = new Dog()`)
**Downcasting**: requires explicit cast, throws `ClassCastException` at runtime if actual type doesn't match — always check with `instanceof` first
**instanceof**: `obj instanceof Type` returns true if obj's actual type IS-A Type (or is Type itself)
